---
title: "Types of variable "
author: "KMK"
date: "2023-10-13"
output: powerpoint_presentation  
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      comment = "",
                      message = FALSE)
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = "D:/docs/Burnet Statistics training/docs")
```

```{r Load, include=FALSE}
getwd()
rm(list = ls())
data <- haven::read_dta("d:/docs/Burnet Statistics training/data_raw/var_type.dta")
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
```

## Variables  
- Characteristics that varies; Not constant
- **Values** Can be text or number
- Mostly used codes for the text values
- Variables in columns 
- Values of observations in row

```{r names, include=F}
names(data)
```

## First seven lines of data from a study of outcome after diagnosis of tuberculosis
```{r table, include=T}
kable(data, col.names = c("ID","Hospital","Date of birth","Gender","Date of diagnosis","Weight(kg)","Smear result","Culture result","Skin test diameter (mm)","Alive after 6 months?","Days of hospital stayed" ,"Completed age at diagnosis", "Age group"),
      align = c("l","r","c","l","c","r","l","l","r","l","r","r","l"))
```

## Types of Variable  
- **Categorical variables**
  + Binary variable
  + Nominal variable
  + Ordinal variable
- **Numerical variables**
  + Discrete variable
  + Continuous variable
  
## Binary variable
```{r binary, include=T}
kable(data[,c(1,8,10)],col.names = c("ID","Culture results","Alive after 6 months"),align = c("l","l","l"))
```

## Nominal variable
```{r nominal, include=T}
data %>% select(1,2,4) %>% kable(col.names = c("ID","Hospital name","Gender"), align = c("l","r","l"))

```
  

## Ordinal variable
```{r ordinal, include=T}
data %>% select(1,7) %>% 
  kable(col.names = c("ID","Smear result"), align = c("l","l"))
```

## Discrete variable
```{r discrete, include=T}
data %>% select(1,11) %>% kable(col.names = c("ID","Days hospitalized"), align = c("l","r"))
```

## Continuous variable
```{r continuous, include=T}
data %>% select(1,6,9,12) %>% kable(col.names = c("ID","Weight in kilogram","Skin test diameter(mm)","Completed age(years)"), align = c("l","r","r","r"))
```

## Classifcation of variable
- can be **numerical** or **categorical**
- includes:
  + Recorded variables
  + Rates
  + Derived variable
  + Transformed variable
  
## Rates
- occurrence over time
- usually follow exponential distribution
- not cover in my topics

## Derived variable
- calculated or categorized from recorded variable
- categorized based on threshold value
- derived from reference curve/ standard values in population

## Calculated or categorized from recorded variable
```{r derived, include=T}
data %>%  select(1,3,5,12,13) %>% kable(align = c("l","c","c","r","l"))
```

## Categorised baased on threshold values

|Derived variables|Original variable|
|:---------------:|:---------------:|
|Low birth-weight|Birth-weight|
|  Yes|  <2500g|
|  No|  >=2500g|
|Vitamin A status|Serum retinol level|
|  Severe deficiency|  <0.35umol/l|
|  Mild/moderate deficiency|  0.35-0.69umol/l|
|  Normal|  >=0.70umol/l|

## Derived from reference curve
![Growth charts for boys\(Image resource : CDC)](d:/docs/Burnet Statistics training/misc/CDC growth chart.png)


## Transformed variable
- Variables with observations that violates assumptions
  * Normality assumption
  * Linear assumption
  * Homogeneity assumption
- Types of transformation
  * Logarithmic ($\log_e{x}$)
  * Reciprocal ($\frac{1}{x}$)
  * Square root ($\sqrt{x}$)
  * Square/cubic ($x^n$)
  
## Distinguishing Outcome and exposure variables  
- To choose appropriate data display and statistical method
- Outcome : seek to understand variation or occurrence
- Exposure : influence size or occurrence of outcome
- Statistical analysis : quantify the magnitude of association between exposures  and outcomes

## Examples of Outcomes and Exposures  

|Outcome|Exposure|
|:------|--------:|
|Baby born with low birth weight (yes, no)| Mother smoked during pregnancy (yes, no)|
|Anthropometric status at 1 year of age (weight-for-age z-score)|Duration of exclusive breastfeeding (weeks)|
|Number of diarrhoea episodes experienced in a year|Access to clean water supply (yes, no)|
|Child develops leukaemia (yes, no)|Proximity to nuclear power station (miles)|
|Survival time (months) following diagnosis of lung cancer|Socio-economic status(6 groups)|

## **Thank you**
